text: I'm Dr. David Lurie of Pasadena, California. In this video, I will discuss using robotic surgery to perform a paraesophageal hiatal hernia repair.
The patient is a sixty-year-old obese male with a body mass index of thirty-one with a two-year history of postprandial vomiting and chest pain.
This has progressed to now occurring on almost a daily basis.
The upper gastrointestinal imaging reveals a predominantly intrathoracic stomach with signs of volvulus, while the gastroesophageal junction is positioned just above the diaphragm.
The patient also had a previous CT scan of the abdomen and chest. This demonstrates also an intrathoracic stomach with no other organs besides the stomach and the omentum up in the chest.
The patient has lost thirty pounds in preparation for surgery.
Upper endoscopy demonstrated a complex parasophageal hiatal hernia with no evidence of ulceration.
Patient also had esophageal manometry performed. This demonstrates a weak lower esophageal sphincter with a pressure of six millimeters of mercury and normal relaxation.
Esophageal body motility demonstrates ineffective peristalsis in approximately forty percent of the swallows.

The XI robotic port placement is indicated in yellow, showing four 8mm ports positioned superior to the umbilicus in a horizontal line. A green assistant 12mm port is triangulated between two of the other ports, positioned more inferiorly. Additionally, a high epigastric trocar is visible for the liver retractor.
The surgical field shows two medial ports and a right lateral port, with the Nathanson liver retractor in position. The camera and three surgical instruments are visibly in place for the procedure.
The surgeon visualizes the pre-pyloric area and distal antrum, with the remainder of the stomach positioned in the chest cavity. The initial step involves reducing the hernia sac.
Do not try and retract the incarcerated stomach.
This will result in serosal injuries and the stomach's going to want to retract back repeatedly into the mediastinum.
The surgeon incises the peritoneum at the attachment to the muscles of the anterior arch of the crura, revealing the mediastinal plane anterior to the hernia sac.
The surgeon uses a mini laparotomy pad to assist in rapid blunt dissection, facilitating the descent of the entire stomach along with the hernia sac from the chest cavity.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel for energy dissection, focusing on the hernia sac reduction with visible precision.
The surgeon carefully preserves the hepatic neurovascular bundle within the abdominal cavity, ensuring these structures remain intact during the procedure.
This will prevent devascularization of the liver in cases of replaced left hepatic arteries and it will preserve the vagal branches to the gallbladder.
The surgeon exposes the right and left cruciate of the diaphragm anteriorly, revealing the mediastinum and providing the first visual glimpse of the esophagus.
The left vagus nerve is visible as it arches away from the esophagus, tented within the areolar tissue attached to the retracted hernia sac.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to tent up the left vagus nerve, fully mobilizing it to remain with the esophagus, visible within the areolar tissue attached to the retracted hernia sac.
The surgeon identifies the end of the vagus nerve as it extends onto the stomach, visible in the inferior portion of the surgical field.

The surgeon demonstrates a maneuver by vertically dividing the anterior surface of the large hiatal hernia sac, exposing the esophagus and vagus nerve. This action facilitates reaching the superior portion of the hernia sac, allowing for further dissection along the superior curve using a laparotomy pad.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer device to remove the final lateral attachments of the hernia sac from the pleura. In the mediastinum, the intact pleura is visible with the lung positioned behind it.
In the abdominal cavity, the surgeon observes the entire stomach descending with a large hernia sac and proceeds to divide the sac away from the stomach.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer device and a harmonic scalpel to divide the short gastric vessels, preparing for a floppy Nissen fundoplication. The vessel sealer is used for larger diameter vessels, while the harmonic scalpel provides precision for finer dissection.
The surgeon demonstrates the use of the improved vessel sealer extend, which is slimmer and faster, for grasping, retracting, sewing, and knot tying during the hernia repair procedure.
The surgeon completes the division of the short gastric vessels encircling the top of the fundus, using surgical instruments to ensure clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon approaches the lesser omentum at the pars flaccida, exposing the right crus of the diaphragm. The perigastric tissues are being separated, with the vena cava visible in the background.

The surgeon creates a large window posterior to the stomach and esophagus, revealing both crura of the diaphragm inferiorly. The posterior vagus nerve is clearly visible during the mediastinal mobilization.

The surgeon lifts the posterior vagus nerve, keeping it elevated alongside the esophagus. A dissection is performed to create a window between the nerve and the aorta, visible just posterior to the dissection site. The circumferential mobilization of the lower esophagus continues.

So of course the purpose of this wide mobilization is to allow ultimately the gastroesophageal junction to rest inferior to the diaphragm in the intra-abdominal cavity without any tension. The theory is to reduce the chance of recurrence or re-herniation.
The likelihood of needing an esophageal lengthening procedure such as a callous gastroplasty is really quite rare when the esophagus is fully mobilized like this.

The surgeon removes the final posterior attachments tethering the esophagus in the mediastinum. A soft Penrose drain loop is passed posterior to the esophagus, positioned just superior to the gastroesophageal junction.

The surgeon holds the two ends of the Penrose drain loop together using an end-of-loop device, facilitating atraumatic retraction of the esophagus. The next step involves closing the diaphragmatic crura.

The surgeon uses an O-ethabon suture, a permanent braided polyester suture, to apply a figure-of-eight pattern across the large curl defect. This technique cinches the defect together in a pulley-like fashion. The intra-abdominal pressure is reduced from fifteen to eight millimeters of mercury to alleviate tension on the closure line.
The surgeon continues the posterior closure behind the esophagus, skillfully maneuvering around the preserved hepatic neurovascular bundle using articulated robotic instruments.

The surgeon plans to reinforce the large hiatal opening closure with mesh, using SH needles to take bites of the peritoneum for added support. Small strips of synthetic mesh are used as buttressing material to prevent tearing under tension.

The surgeon adjusts the direction of the crura closure from a vertical to a hockey stick configuration, moving horizontally to reduce tension and minimize acute angulation of the esophagus as it passes through the diaphragm.

The surgeon uses a bougie sizing device inserted through the esophagus into the stomach to ensure appropriate sizing during the closure. The surgeon then measures the hiatal opening for mesh placement.

The surgeon places a seven by ten centimeter Phasix ST mesh, which is long-term absorbable and has an intestinal barrier coating, along the diaphragm. Absorbable Vicryl sutures are used to secure the mesh in direct apposition. The mesh is tucked behind the left lobe and caudate lobe of the liver for stabilization, with plans to repeat the process on the opposite side.

The surgeon ensures that the bulk of the synthetic mesh overlaps the primary crural closure. Fluid is suctioned out from the surgical field, and the posterior portion of the mesh is carefully tucked behind the spleen, ensuring no fat is present between the mesh and the diaphragm.

The surgeon begins the fundoplication by placing a marking suture on the posterior aspect of the fundus, ensuring it is positioned well behind the greater curvature line where the short gastric vessels were cut. The fundus is passed behind the esophagus, creating a symmetrical wrap without kinks or twists, using the shoeshine maneuver to achieve a loose, floppy wrap. Permanent sutures are used to secure the wrap in place.

The surgeon sutures the right side of the fundus, part of the esophagus, and the left side of the fundus, ensuring the vagus nerve is not entrapped. A third suture is placed between the two edges of the fundus, creating a short two-centimeter floppy wrap. The wrap is then pexed to the undersurface of the diaphragm at multiple points.

The surgeon removes the Penrose retracting loop and the sizing bougie from the surgical field. The final step involves performing gastropexy by selecting a portion on the greater curvature of the stomach and securing it with a strong figure-of-eight suture to the anterior abdominal wall. This completes the robotic-assisted laparoscopic repair of a paraesophageal hiatal hernia.
